steps.title: Use the known angle pair
steps.content: The triangles already have one matching angle pair because angle A and angle P both measure $48^\circ$. To prove similarity by AA, one additional matching angle pair is enough. The side lengths in the choices would need to create a proportional side relationship, but the more direct route is to look for angle information that forces the remaining angles to match.

steps.title: Test the angle information
steps.content:
If angle B measures $37^\circ$ and angle R measures $95^\circ$, the third angle in triangle ABC is:
$$
180^\circ-48^\circ-37^\circ=95^\circ
$$
The third angle in triangle PQR is:
$$
180^\circ-48^\circ-95^\circ=37^\circ
$$
So angle B matches angle Q, and angle C matches angle R.

steps.title: Match the triangles
steps.content: The triangles now have matching angle measures: one pair is the given $48^\circ$ angles, one pair is the $37^\circ$ angles, and one pair is the $95^\circ$ angles. That proves the triangles are similar by AA similarity. The answer is C.
